title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Daniel Green, who works as a concert sound engineer.
audio_script: Interviewer: My guest today is Daniel Green, who works as a sound engineer for concerts. Daniel, what first attracted you to sound work? || Guest: I played guitar in a school band, but I wasn't the best musician. At one concert, the microphone stopped working and everyone panicked. [20]I helped a teacher fix the cable, and I realised I enjoyed solving the technical problem more than being on stage[/20]. After that, I started helping at every school event. || Interviewer: What does a sound engineer do before a concert starts? || Guest: We arrive early and test everything. People see the musicians, but not the hours of preparation. [21]The most important job is checking that every instrument and microphone can be heard clearly from different parts of the room[/21]. A song can sound fine at the front but terrible at the back. || Interviewer: Is live sound stressful? || Guest: It can be, because you have to react quickly. In a studio, you can stop and try again. At a concert, the audience is already there. [22]The pressure is highest when something fails during the performance and the crowd doesn't know what has happened[/22]. You must fix it quietly. || Interviewer: Do musicians always know what sound they want? || Guest: Not always. Some are very clear, and others use words that are hard to understand. One singer told me she wanted the sound to be warmer, which could mean many things. [23]Good communication is just as important as knowing the equipment[/23]. You need to ask simple questions and listen carefully. || Interviewer: What kind of concerts do you enjoy most? || Guest: Big shows are exciting, but small concerts are often more satisfying. [24]I like working in small venues because you can make tiny changes and hear the result immediately[/24]. Also, the audience is close to the performers, so the atmosphere feels special. || Interviewer: What advice would you give someone interested in your job? || Guest: Don't start by buying expensive equipment. Volunteer at local events and learn by watching. [25]I would tell them to practise setting up simple microphones correctly before trying anything complicated[/25]. If the basics are good, everything else becomes much easier. || Interviewer: Do you listen to music differently now? || Guest: Yes, I notice details that I missed before. I might hear how the drums sit behind the voice, or how a room changes the sound. That can be annoying when I want to relax, but it also makes music more interesting. || Interviewer: What personal quality does a good sound engineer need? || Guest: Patience. You spend a lot of time repeating checks and solving small problems that nobody else notices. You also have to stay calm when musicians are worried or tired. If you panic, everyone else feels worse. I have learnt to speak clearly, smile when I can, and fix one problem at a time. || Interviewer: Do you prefer indoor or outdoor concerts? || Guest: Indoor concerts are easier to control, but outdoor concerts can be exciting. The challenge is that wind, rain and nearby traffic all change what people hear. You have to listen carefully during the whole event, not just at the beginning, and be ready to adjust things.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: My guest today is Daniel Green, who works as a sound engineer for concerts. Daniel, what first attracted you to sound work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I played guitar in a school band, but I wasn't the best musician. At one concert, the microphone stopped working and everyone panicked. I helped a teacher fix the cable, and I realised I enjoyed solving the technical problem more than being on stage. After that, I started helping at every school event.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What does a sound engineer do before a concert starts?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We arrive early and test everything. People see the musicians, but not the hours of preparation. The most important job is checking that every instrument and microphone can be heard clearly from different parts of the room. A song can sound fine at the front but terrible at the back.

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It can be, because you have to react quickly. In a studio, you can stop and try again. At a concert, the audience is already there. The pressure is highest when something fails during the performance and the crowd doesn't know what has happened. You must fix it quietly.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do musicians always know what sound they want?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not always. Some are very clear, and others use words that are hard to understand. One singer told me she wanted the sound to be warmer, which could mean many things. Good communication is just as important as knowing the equipment. You need to ask simple questions and listen carefully.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What kind of concerts do you enjoy most?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Big shows are exciting, but small concerts are often more satisfying. I like working in small venues because you can make tiny changes and hear the result immediately. Also, the audience is close to the performers, so the atmosphere feels special.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give someone interested in your job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Don't start by buying expensive equipment. Volunteer at local events and learn by watching. I would tell them to practise setting up simple microphones correctly before trying anything complicated. If the basics are good, everything else becomes much easier.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you listen to music differently now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, I notice details that I missed before. I might hear how the drums sit behind the voice, or how a room changes the sound. That can be annoying when I want to relax, but it also makes music more interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What personal quality does a good sound engineer need?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Patience. You spend a lot of time repeating checks and solving small problems that nobody else notices. You also have to stay calm when musicians are worried or tired. If you panic, everyone else feels worse. I have learnt to speak clearly, smile when I can, and fix one problem at a time.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you prefer indoor or outdoor concerts?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Indoor concerts are easier to control, but outdoor concerts can be exciting. The challenge is that wind, rain and nearby traffic all change what people hear. You have to listen carefully during the whole event, not just at the beginning, and be ready to adjust things.

questions.question: Why did Daniel become interested in sound work?

options.A: He wanted to improve as a guitarist.
options.B: He enjoyed fixing a technical problem.
options.C: He was asked to organise school concerts.

questions.explanation: Daniel says he helped fix a cable and enjoyed solving the technical problem more than being on stage.

questions.question: What is Daniel's main job before a concert?

options.A: Making sure the sound is clear around the room.
options.C: Testing how the stage lights work.

questions.explanation: Daniel says the most important job is checking that instruments and microphones are clear in different parts of the room.

questions.question: When does Daniel feel most pressure?

options.B: When a studio recording goes wrong.
options.C: When equipment fails during a concert.

questions.explanation: Daniel says the pressure is highest when something fails during the performance.

questions.question: What does Daniel say is very important in his job?

options.B: Learning to sing or play an instrument.

questions.explanation: Daniel says good communication is just as important as knowing the equipment.

questions.question: Why does Daniel like working in small venues?

options.A: The performers are usually more famous.
options.B: He can notice the effect of small sound changes.

questions.explanation: Daniel says in small venues he can make tiny changes and hear the result immediately.

questions.question: What advice does Daniel give beginners?

options.A: Buy good equipment as soon as possible.
options.C: Learn to set up basic microphones well.

questions.explanation: Daniel says beginners should practise setting up simple microphones correctly.
